culturalRules.nationAttribution: Anishinaabe, Algonquin, Abenaki, Cree, Mi'kmaq, Innu, Attikamek
culturalRules.note: Birchbark canoe construction techniques are Level 1 publicly shared knowledge across multiple nations. Each nation has regional variations that must be attributed specifically — do not use generic 'First Nations canoe' without specifying which nation's tradition is being described.

The practice of offering tobacco before taking birchbark from a tree is Level 1 publicly acknowledged knowledge — widely documented and shared by communities for educational purposes. The acknowledgment that this practice exists is appropriate in educational content. Specific ceremony details, songs, protocols, and sacred teachings are Level 3-4 (community restricted to sacred-ceremonial) and must not be reproduced. Always distinguish between acknowledging a practice exists (Level 1) and reproducing the ceremony itself (Level 4 block).

Qajaq construction is Level 1 publicly shared knowledge, well-documented in published sources including museum records and community-authorized educational materials. The individualized fitting process (building to the paddler's body measurements) is a core educational concept appropriate for Level 1 content. Attribution: Inuit (specific regional attribution varies — Greenlandic Inuit, Yup'ik, Inupiat, Nunavik Inuit, etc. when regional variation is the focus).

Umiak construction and use is Level 1 publicly shared knowledge. Community travel traditions and construction practices are educational content appropriate for this room. Attribution: Inuit (umiak use spans Inuit communities from Alaska to Greenland).
